title: Optimal Protein Per Meal Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Much Protein Should You Eat Per Meal?
description: Calculate optimal protein per meal for maximum muscle synthesis. Evidence-based recommendations for building muscle. Free & fast.
intro: The Optimal Protein Per Meal Calculator helps you determine exactly how many grams of protein to consume at each sitting to maximize muscle protein synthesis (MPS). Unlike total daily protein—which is relatively well-established at 1.6–2.2 g/kg of body weight for active individuals—per-meal distribution is a more nuanced variable. Research shows that MPS is most efficiently stimulated by doses of approximately 0.24–0.40 g/kg of body weight per meal, meaning a 75 kg person should target roughly 18–30 g of high-quality protein per sitting. Spacing meals 3–5 hours apart allows leucine-triggered anabolic signaling to fully reset before the next dose, preventing "refractory period" losses. This calculator divides your total daily protein goal across your chosen meal frequency and benchmarks each dose against evidence-based per-meal thresholds.
keyTakeaway: **Optimal protein per meal = 0.24–0.40 g × body weight (kg)**; e.g., a 80 kg athlete needs ~19–32 g per meal, spaced at least 3–5 hours apart to fully maximize MPS response at every sitting.
useCases: A 90 kg powerlifter eating 180 g/day wants to know whether splitting across 3 meals (60 g each) vs. 5 meals (36 g each) better matches MPS thresholds.
A 55 kg recreational runner consuming 88 g/day needs to check if her 2-meal eating pattern leaves each dose too large to be efficiently used (~44 g/meal vs. the ~13–22 g optimal range).
A 70 kg person on an intermittent fasting (16:8) protocol fitting all protein into a 4-meal window wants to verify each dose clears the minimum ~17 g MPS threshold.

A sports dietitian designing a post-surgical recovery meal plan for a 65 kg patient needs per-meal protein targets that stimulate MPS without exceeding gut tolerance (~40 g/sitting for most individuals).

faq.q: How much protein can the body absorb in one meal?
faq.a: The idea that the body can only absorb 20–30 g of protein per meal is a myth. The gut absorbs virtually all ingested protein regardless of dose — absorption rate for whey is ~8–10 g/hour, casein ~6 g/hour. The real limit is how much protein per meal maximally stimulates Muscle Protein Synthesis (MPS): research (Moore et al., 2009) places this at roughly 0.24–0.40 g/kg of body weight. Protein beyond that threshold is still absorbed but is preferentially oxidized rather than used for tissue building.

faq.q: Does protein distribution matter as much as total daily intake?
faq.a: Both matter, but for different goals. For general health and weight management, total daily protein (1.2–1.6 g/kg) is the primary lever. For maximizing muscle hypertrophy, distribution across 3–5 meals is a meaningful secondary variable — studies show even distribution can increase MPS by ~25% compared to skewed patterns with the same daily total. For athletes in caloric restriction phases, distribution becomes even more critical to preserving lean mass.

faq.q: What is the optimal number of meals per day for protein utilization?
faq.a: Current evidence supports 3–5 protein-containing meals per day as the practical sweet spot. Fewer than 3 meals often results in per-meal doses that exceed the MPS ceiling (leading to excess oxidation), while more than 5–6 meals provides diminishing returns and is difficult to sustain. A 2012 study by Arciero et al. found that 6 meals/day improved body composition vs. 3 meals at the same daily protein intake in overweight individuals, though differences were modest.

faq.q: How does age affect optimal protein per meal?
faq.a: Older adults (≥65 years) experience 'anabolic resistance' — a blunted MPS response to a given protein dose. Research and the international PROT-AGE consensus recommend at least 25–30 g of high-quality protein per meal for older adults, with some studies supporting up to 0.40–0.60 g/kg per meal (vs. 0.24–0.40 g/kg for younger adults). Leucine supplementation or leucine-enriched protein sources can partially compensate for this resistance.

faq.q: Does the type of protein (whey vs. plant-based) change per-meal recommendations?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. High-quality animal proteins (whey, egg, meat) have DIAAS scores ≥1.0 and are rich in leucine (~8–10%), meaning standard per-meal targets apply. Most plant proteins have lower DIAAS scores (0.5–0.8) and lower leucine density; to hit the ~2–3 g leucine threshold needed to trigger MPS, you may need 35–40% more grams per meal from plant sources. Combining complementary plant proteins (e.g., rice + pea) improves DIAAS closer to animal-protein levels.

faq.q: Does protein timing around workouts change per-meal targets?
faq.a: Post-exercise MPS is elevated for 24–48 hours, making protein more anabolically effective in that window. The 'anabolic window' is wider than once thought (not just 30 minutes but several hours post-training), but consuming 0.3–0.4 g/kg of fast-digesting protein (e.g., whey) within 2 hours post-workout is still considered best practice per the International Society of Sports Nutrition (ISSN) position stand. This meal should be prioritized and counted within your per-meal distribution plan.

faq.q: Is there a risk in eating too much protein per meal?
faq.a: In healthy adults with normal kidney function, no evidence supports harm from high single-meal protein doses. The kidneys adapt to higher protein loads through a process called 'functional reserve.' However, doses >50 g/meal may cause gastrointestinal discomfort (bloating, gas) in some individuals, and excess amino acids will be deaminated and the carbon skeletons used for energy or stored as fat. The NIH and National Academy of Medicine have not set a Tolerable Upper Intake Level (UL) for protein, but practical per-meal efficiency limits remain around 0.40 g/kg.

faq.q: How should I adjust protein per meal during a caloric deficit?
faq.a: During a caloric deficit, total daily protein recommendations increase to 1.8–2.7 g/kg (vs. 1.6–2.2 g/kg for maintenance) to offset muscle-protein breakdown. Per-meal targets scale proportionally: a 75 kg person cutting calories might target 0.40–0.54 g/kg per meal. Distributing this elevated intake across 4–5 meals is especially important in a deficit since lower overall energy availability reduces the efficiency of protein utilization for MPS.

sources.name: NIH – Dietary Reference Intakes for Energy, Carbohydrate, Fiber, Fat, Fatty Acids, Cholesterol, Protein, and Amino Acids (National Academy of Medicine / NIH)
NIH PubMed – Moore DR et al. (2009): Ingested protein dose response of muscle and albumin protein synthesis after resistance exercise in young men
NIH PubMed – Stokes T et al. (2018): Recent Perspectives Regarding the Role of Dietary Protein for the Promotion of Muscle Hypertrophy with Resistance Exercise Training

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calculator applies three sequential formulas:

```
// Step 1 – Protein per meal (simple even distribution)
proteinaPorComida = proteinaDiaria ÷ comidas

// Step 2 – Evidence-based optimal range (Moore et al. 2009; Stokes et al. 2018)
optimo_low  = 0.24 × peso_kg          // ~minimum effective dose
optimo_high = 0.40 × peso_kg          // ~ceiling before diminishing returns

// Step 3 – Meal spacing recommendation
horasEntre = 24 ÷ comidas             // hours between meals (assuming waking hours ≈ 24 h cycle)

// Step 4 – Distribution assessment
if proteinaPorComida < optimo_low  → "Under-dosed – MPS sub-maximally stimulated"
if proteinaPorComida ≤ optimo_high → "Optimal – full MPS response likely"
if proteinaPorComida > optimo_high → "Supra-threshold – excess may be oxidized"
```

The **0.24 g/kg lower bound** comes from dose-response studies showing that below this threshold leucine concentration in plasma is insufficient to trigger maximal MPS. The **0.40 g/kg upper bound** reflects the point beyond which additional protein in a single meal does not proportionally increase MPS in younger adults (though older adults may benefit up to ~0.60 g/kg per meal due to "anabolic resistance").

---

## Reference Table

| Body Weight | Min per Meal (0.24 g/kg) | Max per Meal (0.40 g/kg) | Daily @ 3 meals | Daily @ 4 meals | Daily @ 5 meals |
|-------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 50 kg       | 12 g                     | 20 g                     | 36–60 g         | 48–80 g         | 60–100 g        |
| 60 kg       | 14 g                     | 24 g                     | 43–72 g         | 58–96 g         | 72–120 g        |
| 70 kg       | 17 g                     | 28 g                     | 50–84 g         | 67–112 g        | 84–140 g        |
| 80 kg       | 19 g                     | 32 g                     | 58–96 g         | 77–128 g        | 96–160 g        |
| 90 kg       | 22 g                     | 36 g                     | 65–108 g        | 86–144 g        | 108–180 g        |
| 100 kg      | 24 g                     | 40 g                     | 72–120 g        | 96–160 g        | 120–200 g       |

*Daily totals shown are the aggregated per-meal optimal range × number of meals, for illustrative purposes only.*

---

## Typical Cases

**Case 1 – Recreational gym-goer (75 kg, 150 g/day, 4 meals)**
- Per meal: 150 ÷ 4 = **37.5 g**
- Optimal range: 0.24×75 = 18 g → 0.40×75 = 30 g
- Assessment: **Supra-threshold** (37.5 g > 30 g). Surplus ~7.5 g/meal will likely be oxidized for energy. Consider adding a 5th meal or reducing daily total slightly if fat loss is the goal.

**Case 2 – 58 kg female endurance athlete (110 g/day, 5 meals)**
- Per meal: 110 ÷ 5 = **22 g**
- Optimal range: 0.24×58 = 13.9 g → 0.40×58 = 23.2 g
- Assessment: **Optimal** — 22 g sits squarely within the 14–23 g window. Spacing meals every ~4–5 hours keeps the anabolic signal firing consistently.

**Case 3 – 85 kg older adult (≥65 yrs, 136 g/day, 3 meals)**
- Per meal: 136 ÷ 3 = **~45 g**
- Adjusted optimal range for older adults: up to 0.60×85 = **51 g** (anabolic resistance requires higher doses)
- Assessment: **Adequate** for an older adult. This aligns with PROT-AGE guidelines recommending 25–30 g minimum per meal for seniors.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Skipping breakfast protein** — Concentrating 70%+ of daily protein in dinner creates a "pulse" pattern where most protein is oxidized rather than used for MPS. Even distribution across meals is consistently superior in research.

2. **Ignoring protein quality (DIAAS score)** — 30 g of pea protein (DIAAS ~0.64) is not equivalent to 30 g of whey (DIAAS ~1.09). Doses should be adjusted upward for incomplete protein sources; the per-meal formulas above assume high-quality, leucine-rich proteins.

3. **Applying adult thresholds to older adults** — Adults over 65 have "anabolic resistance," meaning MPS requires higher per-meal doses (~0.40–0.60 g/kg) to achieve the same response as a younger adult at 0.24–0.40 g/kg. Using younger-adult targets for seniors results in chronic under-stimulation of MPS.

4. **Conflating total daily protein with per-meal distribution** — Hitting 160 g/day in two meals of 80 g does not produce the same anabolic outcome as 4 meals of 40 g. The refractory period of MPS (~3 hours post-stimulation) means large, infrequent doses waste much of the surplus.

5. **Ignoring leucine threshold** — A meal must deliver roughly **2–3 g of leucine** to trigger maximal MPS. Foods low in leucine (many plant proteins) may meet the gram target while failing the leucine threshold. Leucine content: whey ~10%, egg ~8.5%, soy ~7.8%, wheat ~6.8%.
